votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the cyclic encoding and decoding logic, processing elements in chunks of 3 and leaving the remainder unchanged, exactly matching the Coq specification.
The Python code correctly implements the cyclic encoding and decoding logic by processing the list in chunks of 3 and shifting the elements exactly as specified in the Coq code.
The Python code correctly implements the cyclic encoding and decoding logic by processing the list in chunks of 3, matching the Coq pattern matching behavior.

The Python code correctly implements the same cyclic encoding/decoding logic as the Coq code. For encode_cyclic_list, both take groups of 3 elements [c1,c2,c3] and produce [c2,c3,c1]. For decode_cyclic_list, both take groups of 3 elements [c1,c2,c3] and produce [c3,c1,c2]. Remaining elements (fewer than 3) are preserved unchanged. The string_to_list and list_to_string conversions work equivalently between Coq ascii lists and Python character lists. The spec functions correctly compare the result against the expected transformation.

The Python code correctly implements the same cyclic encoding/decoding logic as the Coq code. For encode_cyclic_list, both take groups of 3 elements [c1,c2,c3] and produce [c2,c3,c1]. For decode_cyclic_list, both take [c1,c2,c3] and produce [c3,c1,c2]. Remaining elements (less than 3) are preserved unchanged. The string_to_list and list_to_string conversions work equivalently between Coq ascii lists and Python character lists. The spec functions correctly check equality of the result with the expected encoded/decoded string.

The Python code correctly implements the same cyclic encoding/decoding logic as the Coq code. For encode_cyclic_list, both take groups of 3 elements [c1,c2,c3] and reorder to [c2,c3,c1]. For decode_cyclic_list, both take [c1,c2,c3] and reorder to [c3,c1,c2]. The Python while loop with condition 'i + 2 < len(l)' correctly handles groups of 3 (equivalent to matching c1::c2::c3::rest), and remaining elements (0, 1, or 2) are appended unchanged, matching the Coq '_ => l' case. The spec functions compare results identically.

Both implementations convert strings to lists, cyclically permute each triple (c1,c2,c3) -> (c2,c3,c1) for encode and -> (c3,c1,c2) for decode, leave leftovers unchanged, and convert back. Python mirrors Coq’s behavior on all concrete inputs.

Both Coq and Python rotate each consecutive triple [c1,c2,c3] to [c2,c3,c1] for encode and to [c3,c1,c2] for decode, leaving 1–2 trailing elements unchanged; string/list conversions are consistent, so results match on all concrete inputs.

Both implementations rotate characters in groups of three (c1,c2,c3)->(c2,c3,c1) for encode and (c1,c2,c3)->(c3,c1,c2) for decode, leaving 1–2 trailing chars unchanged. The string/list conversions are consistent, so the specs compare the same results.
